title: A Death In The Desert
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ [Supposed of Pamphylax the Antiochene:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is a parchment, of my rolls the fifth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hath three skins glued together, is all Greek,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And goeth from Epsilon down to Mu:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lies second in the surnamed Chosen Chest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stained and conserved with juice of terebinth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Covered with cloth of hair, and lettered Xi,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From Xanthus, my wifeן¿½s uncle, now at peace:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mu and Epsilon stand for my own name.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I may not write it, but I make a cross
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To show I wait His coming, with the rest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leave off here: beginneth Pamphylax.]

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said, ן¿½If one should wet his lips with wine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And slip the broadest plantain-leaf we find,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or else the lappet of a linen robe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Into the water-vessel, lay it right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And cool his forehead just above the eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The while a brother, kneeling either side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Should chafe each hand and try to make it warm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He is not so far gone but he might speak.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This did not happen in the outer cave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor in the secret chamber of the rock
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where, sixty days since the decree was out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We had him, bedded on a camel-skin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And waited for his dying all the while;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in the midmost grotto: since noonן¿½s light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Reached there a little, and we would not lose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The last of what might happen on his face.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I at the head, and Xanthus at the feet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Valens and the Boy, had lifted him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And brought him from the chamber in the depths,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And laid him in the light where we might see:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For certain smiles began about his mouth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his lids moved, presageful of the end.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beyond, and half way up the mouth oן¿½ the cave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Bactrian convert, having his desire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Kept watch, and made pretence to graze a goat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That gave us milk, on rags of various herb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Plantain and quitch, the rocksן¿½ shade keeps alive:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So that if any thief or soldier passed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Because the persecution was aware,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yielding the goat up promptly with his life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such man might pass on, joyful at a prize,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor care to pry into the cool oן¿½ the cave.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Outside was all noon and the burning blue.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Here is wine,ן¿½ answered Xanthus, dropped a drop;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I stooped and placed the lap of cloth aright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then chafed his right hand, and the Boy his left:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Valens had bethought him, and produced
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And broke a ball of nard, and made perfume.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only, he did not so much wake, as turn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And smile a little, as a sleeper does
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If any dear one call him, touch his face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And smiles and loves, but will not be disturbed.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Xanthus said a prayer, but still he slept:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is the Xanthus that escaped to Rome,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was burned, and could not write the chronicle.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the Boy sprang up from his knees, and ran,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stung by the splendour of a sudden thought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fetched the seventh plate of graven lead
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of the secret chamber, found a place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pressing with finger on the deeper dints,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And spoke, as ן¿½t were his mouth proclaiming first,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am the Resurrection and the Life.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereat he opened his eyes wide at once,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sat up of himself, and looked at us;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thenceforth nobody pronounced a word:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only, outside, the Bactrian cried his cry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like the lone desert-bird that wears the ruff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As signal we were safe, from time to time.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ First he said, ן¿½If a friend declared to me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This my son Valens, this my other son,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Were James and Peter, nay, declared as well
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This lad was very John, I could believe!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Could, for a moment, doubtlessly believe:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So is myself withdrawn into my depths,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The soul retreated from the perished brain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whence it was wont to feel and use the world
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Through these dull members, done with long ago.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet I myself remain; I feel myself:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And there is nothing lost. Let be, awhile!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ [This is the doctrine he was wont to teach,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How divers persons witness in each man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Three souls which make up one soul: first, to wit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A soul of each and all the bodily parts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seated therein, which works, and is what Does,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And has the use of earth, and ends the man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Downward: but, tending upward for advice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grows into, and again is grown into
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the next soul, which, seated in the brain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Useth the first with its collected use,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And feeleth, thinketh, willeth, is what Knows:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which, duly tending upward in its turn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grows into, and again is grown into
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the last soul, that uses both the first,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Subsisting whether they assist or no,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, constituting manן¿½s self, is what Is
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leans upon the former, makes it play,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As that played off the first: and, tending up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Holds, is upheld by, God, and ends the man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upward in that dread point of intercourse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor needs a place, for it returns to Him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What Does, what Knows, what Is; three souls, one man.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I give the glossa of Theotypas.]

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then, ן¿½A stick, once fire from end to end;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now, ashes save the tip that holds a spark!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet, blow the spark, it runs back, spreads itself
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A little where the fire was: thus I urge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The soul that served me, till it task once more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What ashes of my brain have kept their shape,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And these make effort on the last oן¿½ the flesh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Trying to taste again the truth of things ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (He smiled) ן¿½their very superficial truth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As that ye are my sons, that it is long
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Since James and Peter had release by death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And I am only he, your brother John,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who saw and heard, and could remember all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Remember all! It is not much to say.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What if the truth broke on me from above
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As once and oft-times? Such might hap again:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Doubtlessly He might stand in presence here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With head wool-white, eyes flame, and feet like brass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The sword and the seven stars, as I have seen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I who now shudder only and surmise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How did your brother bear that sight and live?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If I live yet, it is for good, more love
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Through me to men: be nought but ashes here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That keep awhile my semblance, who was John,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Still, when they scatter, there is left on earth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No one alive who knew (consider this!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Saw with his eyes and handled with his hands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That which was from the first, the Word of Life.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How will it be when none more saith ן¿½I sawן¿½?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Such ever was loveן¿½s way: to rise, it stoops.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Since I, whom Christן¿½s mouth taught, was bidden teach,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I went, for many years, about the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Saying ן¿½It was so; so I heard and saw,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Speaking as the case asked: and men believed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Afterward came the message to myself
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½In Patmos isle; I was not bidden teach,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But simply listen, take a book and write,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Nor set down other than the given word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With nothing left to my arbitrament
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To choose or change: I wrote, and men believed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then, for my time grew brief, no message more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No call to write again, I found a way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, reasoning from my knowledge, merely taught
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Men should, for loveן¿½s sake, in loveן¿½s strength believe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or I would pen a letter to a friend
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And urge the same as friend, nor less nor more:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Friends said I reasoned rightly, and believed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But at the last, why, I seemed left alive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Like a sea jelly weak on Patmos strand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To tell dry sea-beach gazers how I fared
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When there was mid-sea, and the mighty things;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Left to repeat, ן¿½I saw, I heard, I knew,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And go all over the old ground again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With Antichrist already in the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And many Antichrists, who answered prompt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Am I not Jasper as thyself art John?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Nay, young, whereas through age thou mayest forget;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Wherefore, explain, or how shall we believe?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I never thought to call down fire on such,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or, as in wonderful and early days,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Pick up the scorpion, tread the serpent dumb;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But patient stated much of the Lordן¿½s life
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Forgotten or misdelivered, and let it work:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Since much that at the first, in deed and word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lay simply and sufficiently exposed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Had grown (or else my soul was grown to match,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Fed through such years, familiar with such light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Guarded and guided still to see and speak)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of new significance and fresh result;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What first were guessed as points, I now knew stars,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And named them in the Gospel I have writ.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For men said, ן¿½It is getting long ago:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Where is the promise of His coming?ן¿½ asked
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½These young ones in their strength, as loth to wait,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of me who, when their sires were born, was old.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I, for I loved them, answered, joyfully,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Since I was there, and helpful in my age;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, in the main, I think such men believed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Finally, thus endeavouring, I fell sick,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye brought me here, and I supposed the end,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And went to sleep with one thought that, at least,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Though the whole earth should lie in wickedness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We had the truth, might leave the rest to God.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet now I wake in such decrepitude
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As I had slidden down and fallen afar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Past even the presence of my former self,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Grasping the while for stay at facts which snap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Till I am found away from my own world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Feeling for foot-hold through a blank profound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Along with unborn people in strange lands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who say I hear said or conceive they say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Was John at all, and did he say he saw?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Assure us, ere we ask what he might see!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And how shall I assure them? Can they share
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They, who have flesh, a veil of youth and strength
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½About each spirit, that needs must bide its time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Living and learning still as years assist
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Which wear the thickness thin, and let man see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With me who hardly am withheld at all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But shudderingly, scarce a shred between,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lie bare to the universal prick of light?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is it for nothing we grow old and weak,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We whom God loves? When pain ends, gain ends too.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To me, that story ay, that Life and Death
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of which I wrote ן¿½it wasן¿½ to me, it is;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is, here and now: I apprehend nought else.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is not God now iן¿½ the world His power first made?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is not His love at issue still with sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Visibly when a wrong is done on earth?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Love, wrong, and pain, what see I else around?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yea, and the Resurrection and Uprise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To the right hand of the throne what is it beside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When such truth, breaking bounds, oן¿½erfloods my soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, as I saw the sin and death, even so
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½See I the need yet transiency of both,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The good and glory consummated thence?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I saw the power; I see the Love, once weak,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Resume the Power: and in this word ן¿½I see,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lo, there is recognized the Spirit of both
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That moving oן¿½er the spirit of man, unblinds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½His eye and bids him look. These are, I see;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But ye, the children, His beloved ones too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye need, as I should use an optic glass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I wondered at erewhile, somewhere iן¿½ the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½It had been given a crafty smith to make;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A tube, he turned on objects brought too close,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lying confusedly insubordinate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For the unassisted eye to master once:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Look through his tube, at distance now they lay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Become succinct, distinct, so small, so clear!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Just thus, ye needs must apprehend what truth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I see, reduced to plain historic fact,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Diminished into clearness, proved a point
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And far away: ye would withdraw your sense
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From out eternity, strain it upon time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then stand before that fact, that Life and Death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Stay there at gaze, till it dispart, dispread,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As though a star should open out, all sides,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Grow the world on you, as it is my world.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For life, with all it yields of joy and woe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And hope and fear, believe the aged friend,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is just our chance oן¿½ the prize of learning love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How love might be, hath been indeed, and is;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And that we hold thenceforth to the uttermost
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Such prize despite the envy of the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, having gained truth, keep truth: that is all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But see the double way wherein we are led,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How the soul learns diversely from the flesh!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With flesh, that hath so little time to stay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And yields mere basement for the soulן¿½s emprise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Expect prompt teaching. Helpful was the light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And warmth was cherishing and food was choice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To every manן¿½s flesh, thousand years ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As now to yours and mine; the body sprang
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½At once to the height, and stayed: but the soul, no!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Since sages who, this noontide, meditate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½In Rome or Athens, may descry some point
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of the eternal power, hid yestereve;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, as thereby the powerן¿½s whole mass extends,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So much extends the ן¿½ther floating oן¿½er,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The love that tops the might, the Christ in God.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then, as new lessons shall be learned in these
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Till earthן¿½s work stop and useless time run out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So duly, daily, needs provision be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For keeping the soulן¿½s prowess possible,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Building new barriers as the old decay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Saving us from evasion of lifeן¿½s proof,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Putting the question ever, ן¿½Does God love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½And will ye hold that truth against the world?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye know there needs no second proof with good
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gained for our flesh from any earthly source:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We might go freezing, ages, give us fire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thereafter we judge fire at its full worth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And guard it safe through every chance, ye know!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That fable of Prometheus and his theft,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How mortals gained Joveן¿½s fiery flower, grows old
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½(I have been used to hear the pagans own)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And out of mind; but fire, howeן¿½er its birth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Here is it, precious to the sophist now
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who laughs the myth of ן¿½schylus to scorn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As precious to those satyrs of his play,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who touched it in gay wonder at the thing.
